title: provisioning-catalog.v1 — the optional-module catalog
description: The committed catalog of the engine's optional modules — the ones a non-engineer can choose to add — that provisioning maintains and two readers relay: the first-run setup walkthrough (which presents them, grouped by discipline, as opt-out-able choices) and the /engine-help command index (which shows an uninstalled module's command as 'available if you install it'). It records presentation data only — a one-line plain-language gloss, the discipline it belongs to, and, for a command-bearing module, the command an operator would type — keyed by module id; it is not the module manifest and decides nothing about what is installed. An optional module that adds no operator command (fired by a gate, not typed) carries no command and is still offered at setup by its description. It lives at .engine/provisioning/module-catalog.json and is a top-level array. It ships empty and grows as optional modules are built. This schema governs its shape only.

id.description: The module's stable identifier, matching its module folder (.engine/modules/<id>/). Lower-case letters, digits, and hyphens.

verb.description: Optional. The command an operator would type to use this module once it is installed (for example engine-board-setup), shown without the leading slash. This is what /engine-help lists under 'available if you install it'. Present only for command-bearing modules; an optional module that adds no operator command (for example a review-lens module fired by the build gate, never typed) omits it, and the first-run setup walkthrough still offers it by its plain-language description.

description.description: One plain-language sentence answering what the module does and who it is for. It is shown to the operator in the setup walkthrough and in /engine-help, so it must read in plain language with no engine jargon.

category.description: The software-discipline group the module is presented under in the setup walkthrough (recognized SDLC umbrellas). One of the three fixed groups.

status.description: How the module is carried, mirroring the module manifest's status for the opt-out-able set: optional (added only if chosen), default-on (added unless the operator opts out), or experimental (opt-in, not yet stable). Absent is treated as optional.
